description: To be born; birth as event and as noun. The first threshold of the life-arc, partnered with 'lumeo' (die/death) at the other end. The word carries its own teaching: its heart is the syllable 'wi' (two) — birth is the moment one life becomes two, the mother and the child, the world and its newest member.
sound_symbolism: The wisdom-breath 'tho' opens the passage, the pair-syllable 'wi' holds the moment of becoming-two, and the fully open 'a' is arrival itself — the first cry, the open air. The vowels travel from the rounded interior 'o' through the focused 'i' to the widest openness: the whole journey of being born, sounded. Shares almost nothing with 'lumeo' (die), so the two great thresholds can never be mistaken for each other.
grammatical_notes: Verb: 'lopia to thowia' (the child was born — past marked with 'to'). As a noun, birth itself: 'thia thowia philo' (the day of your birth). The sutta phrase 'rena wea lioru shua' (those coming toward life) remains available as conscious poetry for the not-yet-born.

pillars.secular-buddhist-philosophy: Impermanence begins here: everything that arises, arises. Naming birth plainly honors the first noble fact of any life — that it started, and so will end. The word stands with 'lumeo' as the two thresholds mindfulness must be able to speak.
pillars.pre-industrial-wisdom: Birth was the village's central event — attended, sung, counted by seasons. A language of embodied life names its beginning without euphemism.

tags.community: The arrival of a new member
tags.nature: The universal beginning of living things
tags.temporal: The first threshold of a life
